attributes.stem:
A 66-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of worsening abdominal pain and distension, with some early mild confusion. He has a history of progressively worsening liver failure and has recently been referred to the palliative care service, as he is not a candidate for liver transplantation. In the past three weeks, he has had multiple presentations with decompensated liver disease, requiring recurrent drainage of ascitic fluid for comfort. He has a transient living situation and is currently homeless. He has no other significant medical history. His current medications include lactulose and spironolactone.

On examination, he appears unwell and uncomfortable. His pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. His abdomen is grossly distended with a positive fluid thrill and shifting dullness. There is generalised tenderness on palpation but no guarding or rigidity.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms of refractory ascites in the context of end-stage liver disease. Given his recent referral to palliative care and multiple recent presentations for ascitic fluid drainage, the most appropriate next step in management is to perform large-volume paracentesis. This procedure can provide significant symptom relief by reducing abdominal distension and pain, improving the patient's comfort and quality of life, which are primary goals in palliative care.

Given repeated paracentesis, referral for a tunnelled, indwelling peritoneal catheter should also be considered for definitive management. This may require an interventional radiology or gastroenterology service however, so initial symptom based treatment should take precedence.

Indwelling peritoneal catheter insertion or long term abdominal drains (LTAD), are a more invasive option that might be considered for management of refractory ascites in some palliative care patients, particularly for malignant ascites. However, it carries risks of infection and complications, and will require ongoing care and management by the patient, and is not standard of care for ascites secondary to liver cirrhosis. Patients will need to be supported with education and training in how to manage the drain and do simple dressing care, but in the right circumstances this intervention can improve quality of life and avoid repeated hospital presentations for drainage of ascites. Ideally referral for this procedure should come from a multidisciplinary team recommendation, the patients social situation must be considered in order to optimise management for this invasive device.

Administering intravenous albumin is often done in conjunction with large-volume paracentesis to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction. However, in a palliative care setting where the focus is on symptom relief rather than long-term management, this may not be necessary, especially if the patient has undergone multiple recent procedures without complications.

Increasing oral diuretic doses is unlikely to provide rapid symptom relief in this situation. While diuretics can be useful in managing ascites in earlier stages of liver disease, they are often ineffective in end-stage disease with refractory ascites. Moreover, they may take days to show effect and could potentially worsen the patient's electrolyte imbalances.

Starting prophylactic antibiotics is not indicated in this scenario. While patients with cirrhosis and ascites are at increased risk of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, there are no signs of infection presented in this case. Prophylactic antibiotics are typically reserved for specific high-risk situations and are not routinely given with each paracentesis.

In end-stage liver disease managed palliatively, the focus shifts from treating the underlying condition to managing symptoms and improving quality of life. Large-volume paracentesis, even if required frequently, can provide significant relief and is an appropriate intervention in this context.
learningPoints: Palliative paracentesis can provide significant symptom relief for patients with end-stage liver disease and refractory ascites.
In end-stage liver disease, the focus of care shifts to symptom management and quality of life rather than curative treatments.
Frequent large-volume paracentesis can be appropriate in palliative care settings, even if it may not address the underlying disease process.
Indwelling peritoneal catheter insertion for refractory ascites should be considered on a case by case basis, in consultation with a multidisciplinary team.
references: Harvey JJ, Prentice R, George J. Diagnostic and therapeutic abdominal paracentesis. The Medical Journal of Australia. 2023 Jan;218(1):18. Available online at ; https://www.mja.com.au/journal/2023/218/1/diagnostic-and-therapeutic-abdominal-paracentesis#15

Macken L, Corrigan M, Prentice W, Finlay F, McDonagh J, Rajoriya N, Salmon C, Donnelly M, Evans C, Ganai B, Bedlington J. Palliative long-term abdominal drains for the management of refractory ascites due to cirrhosis: a consensus document. Frontline Gastroenterology. 2022 Aug 1;13(e1):e116-25. Available online at : https://fg.bmj.com/content/flgastro/13/e1/e116.full.pdf
